articles.title: Discussion about C array type semantics
articles.summary: C array types are distinct from pointer types in terms of how they're used and interpreted. An array type T[n] represents a contiguous sequence of T values, but you can't directly refer to these values. Instead, any expression that would be of this type is converted to a pointer type T*. This conversion happens when the array indexing operator arr[ix] operates on pointers, allowing for treating arrays like pointers. In contrast, functions are treated differently and don't decay to their first argument when passed as an array or function value.

The research team's findings have significant implications for reducing aerodynamic drag in high-speed aircraft, automobiles, and bullet trains, offering a new approach to minimizing energy consumption at high speeds.

articles.title: Defeating Git Rigour Fatigue with Jujutsu
articles.summary: Defeating Git Rigour Fatigue with Jujutsu Computers This post assumes a basic level of familiarity with the jujutsu version control system. When developing a large feature, writing Good Commits is hard and by Good Commits, I mean something like define types add DB functions server CRUD client API client UI. Jujutsu makes it easier to hop around commits and iterate quickly on compartmentalized changesets, but it's still effortful and I get averse. jj absorb helps somewhat, as does jj squash -i, but they both have their downsides: absorb assigns the changes based on whichever previous commit most recently touched those files, which sometimes doesn't actually correspond to which commit should own these particular changes.

articles.title: libwce: the entropy layer of a wavelet codec, on its own
articles.summary: libwce is a bare-bones entropy layer implementation that compresses wavelet coefficients by estimating their bit-plane count from neighboring groups, rather than tracking individual pixels like older codecs. This approach allows for more efficient compression of similar color and brightness values in the image, resulting in smaller file sizes while maintaining acceptable quality. By using BPC coding with groups of four coefficients at a time, libwce can compress wavelet coefficients down to a small number of significant bits, making it suitable for applications where low-bitrate video is required.

articles.title: Constraint Decay: The Fragility of LLM Agents in Back End Code Generation
articles.summary: Large Language Model agents demonstrate strong performance in autonomous code generation under loose specifications, but production-grade software requires strict adherence to structural constraints such as architectural patterns databases and object-relational mappings. A systematic study evaluating how well agents handle these non-functional requirements reveals a phenomenon of constraint decay where agent performance declines substantially with increasing structural complexity. This challenges the key open challenge for coding agents to jointly satisfy functional and structural requirements.

articles.title: On the (2021)
articles.summary: The description list element is underrated as it's used to represent a list of name-value pairs and is incredibly versatile, making it suitable for various UI patterns such as listing lodging amenities or individual charges in monthly rent. The anatomy of the description list includes three separate elements: <dl>, <dt> for the name-value pair, and <dd> for the detail. This semantic way to mark up a list of name-value groups is essential for developers who want to create accessible and user-friendly interfaces.

articles.title: Jira is Turing-Complete
articles.summary: Jira is Turing-Complete, a claim that has been folklore for some time, is now being proven through a proof of its computational model, which uses two unbounded counters and finite set of labeled instructions to simulate the Minsky register machine, a Turing-complete model established by Minsky in 1967. This achievement demonstrates Jira's automation language can reduce any program into this model, making it Turing-complete.

articles.summary: Don't Roll Your Own Crypto: Why You Should Use Established Tools Instead of Implementing Cryptography Yourself. By Susam Pal on 23 May 2026 Many software developers and security-sensitive system workers are advised to never roll their own crypto, but the advice is especially relevant when implementing cryptography in software or services that serve others. This includes using established software packages or tools for cryptographic tasks such as encryption, decryption, and key management.
